(8) Learning Hexo from Scratch - Basic npm Commands You Should Know
Let's go through the npm commands you really need to know.
You don't use this one a lot, but if you ever hit something weird or an error, you'll need this great command — because you'll want to feed your current npm version and error message into Google:
When you create a new project, run this command first. If you want to install anything from npm later, this needs to come first — otherwise you'll get errors:
it'll ask you for project info. If you don't feel like typing all that, you can skip it with:
npm will then fill everything in with defaults.
file, which stores the names and versions of packages you download from npm.
We didn't run this earlier because Hexo already prepped this for us when creating the project.
Now let's talk about installing packages. There's a major gotcha here too — "local install" vs "project install". These two are really easy to confuse, so I'll walk through both.
We've actually already used the "local install" command in an earlier post:
"Local" here means your computer — you're installing the package onto your machine, taking up your local space. Packages installed this way let you type commands directly in your terminal, like
. Without the install, the terminal won't recognise these commands.
, and you can put it before or after the package name — there's no hard rule:

npm install -g hexo-cli
npm install hexo-cli -g

Personally I put it in front. You might be wondering how to tell which packages need a local install — for that, you'll need to open your eyes and read the official docs, since they'll usually tell you exactly what to type.
A project install just puts the package inside your current project — I know that sounds like a tautology, so put another way: the package gets installed into
the folder of the current project
. This is different from local install. The package gets recorded in
, so it moves with the project folder.
to record the package info in
. We already did this in the previous post:
before or after, no hard rule.
By the way, npm packages also have two categories:
These two project install modes are slightly different. In short,
are packages your project still needs after production or build — things like jQuery, swiper, etc. If a package will be used in production, it goes in
are packages you only use during development — Babel, ESLint, that kind of thing. They only matter during dev.
In practice, official docs usually tell you which one to use. If you genuinely have no idea, just dump everything into
, and you can re-organise later once you understand the difference.

Last one — restoring packages. Earlier I mentioned that npm packages installed into a project get tracked in
, with the actual files cached in
. That folder is usually huge — anywhere from several MB to over a GB.
So when you transfer the project or switch machines, you don't include
in version control or upload it. If you did, there's a high chance things would break — npm installs OS-specific stuff, so
can't be version-controlled or uploaded, how do you restore packages on a new machine?
to restore them. And the magic command for this is
the great sutra of perfect wisdom...
folder comes back to take up your disk space again, and the project runs fine.
